stimulus:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

  • Two competing theories of magnetism divided natural philosophers for centuries.
  • The ancient Greek philosopher Thales (circa 600 BCE) endorsed an animistic theory.
  • That theory held that lodestones contain a kind of soul or life-force that attracts iron.
  • The Roman writer Lucretius (circa 50 BCE) endorsed a corpuscular theory.
  • That theory held that lodestones emit invisible particles that mechanically push and pull iron objects.
  • In the 1600s, William Gilbert's experiments largely settled the debate in favor of a non-animistic, field-based account.
stem: The student wants to provide a historical overview of the two theories. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

answerOptions.content: Through two competing theories of magnetism—animistic and corpuscular—Thales, Lucretius, and Gilbert held that lodestones either contain a life-force or emit invisible particles.

Natural philosophers were divided between the animistic and corpuscular theories of magnetism until William Gilbert's seventeenth-century experiments largely settled the debate in favor of a non-animistic, field-based account.
While Gilbert largely settled the debate in the 1600s, Lucretius supported the corpuscular theory centuries before.
Before the 1600s, the ancient Greek philosopher Thales supported the corpuscular theory, which held that lodestones emit invisible particles that pull iron objects.

rationale:
Choice B is the best answer because it walks through the historical arc: a long debate between two named theories, resolved by Gilbert's experiments.

Choice A mishandles the theories' content. Choice C gives only a fragment of the history. Choice D misattributes the corpuscular theory to Thales.
